[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Target Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Target Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Target Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>LABEL</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded only in observable patterns from the labeled examples, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature, feature value
- observable feature, feature value
- observable feature, feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
  referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type, cat
- fur color, black
- eye color, yellow/gold
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = black] THEN [Bombay]
- IF [animal type = dog] THEN [American Bulldog]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for Bombay fires because the image shows a cat with black fur and yellow eyes, matching the pattern seen in the Bombay example.
</rule_check>

<response>Bombay</response>

# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Target Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Target Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Target Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>LABEL</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded only in observable patterns from the labeled examples, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature, feature value
- observable feature, feature value
- observable feature, feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
  referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type, dog
- coat color, white with brown patches
- facial features, large snout, dark nose, floppy ears
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = cat AND coat color = black] THEN [Bombay]
- IF [animal type = dog AND coat color = white/brown patches] THEN [American Bulldog]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for [American Bulldog] fires because the image shows a dog with white fur and brown patches around the eyes and ears, matching the pattern seen in the American Bulldog example.
</rule_check>

<response>American Bulldog</response>
